description: The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ defines a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material using the coincident site lattice (CSL) model. In this notation, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, which is a positive integer. The Miller indices $(1 1 3)$ denote the crystallographic plane that forms the boundary plane between the two misoriented crystals. The relationship $\Sigma = 11$ indicates that the volume of the CSL unit cell is 11 times larger than that of the primitive crystal lattice cell, characterizing a particular geometric misorientation between the two adjoining grains. This specific CSL description corresponds to a misorientation angle of approximately $\theta = 50.48^\circ$, as noted in the provided context, bounding one end of the misorientation range investigated in the study.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ describes a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, a parameter used to classify symmetric tilt boundaries. The notation $1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ specifies the boundary's crystallographic orientation, where $(1 1 3)$ represents the Miller indices of the boundary plane. This particular $\Sigma 11$ boundary is identified as a special low-energy grain boundary due to its atomic structure, which consists of close-packed structural units forming a capped-trigonal prism configuration.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material, where the symbol $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the boundary. The notation $3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ specifies the geometric parameters of this boundary: the integer $3$ is the sigma value ($\Sigma$), which quantifies the degree of lattice coincidence, while the Miller indices $(1 1 1)$ indicate the crystallographic plane in which the boundary lies. This relationship characterizes a symmetric tilt grain boundary with a high degree of atomic coincidence, resulting in minimal distortion of the perfect crystal lattice on either side of the interface, as evidenced by the structural units (SUs) shown in the corresponding figure.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes the crystallographic notation for a specific grain boundary, where the sigma symbol ($\sum$) denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites. In this notation, the integer 3 represents the sigma value, which quantifies the degree of lattice coincidence, while the Miller indices $(1 1 1)$ specify the boundary plane. This relationship indicates that the boundary is a $\sum 3$ coherent twin boundary lying on the $\{1 1 1\}$ plane, a configuration characterized by minimal lattice distortion and a high density of coincident atomic sites across the interface.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ defines a crystallographic parameter, where $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites for a grain boundary. In this notation, the numerical ratio specifies the orientation relationship between two adjacent crystalline grains. Specifically, the expression describes a boundary characterized by a $\Sigma 11$ coincidence site lattice (CSL), with the boundary plane oriented along the $(113)$ crystallographic direction in one of the grains. This parameter quantifies the degree of geometric alignment and periodicity at the interface, which influences the boundary's energy and structural properties.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes a specific crystallographic orientation relationship, where the symbol $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites at a grain boundary. In this notation, the integer 3 represents the sigma value, which quantifies the degree of lattice coincidence, while the crystallographic indices $(1 1 1)$ specify the boundary plane. This relationship indicates that the boundary is a coherent twin boundary on the {111} plane within a cubic crystal system, characterized by a precise misorientation that produces a high density of atomic site matching. The equation thus formally defines the geometric parameters of a symmetric tilt grain boundary.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ denotes a specific grain boundary characterization, where the symbol $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the interface between two crystalline grains. In this notation, the integer 3 is the sigma value, which quantifies the periodicity of the atomic coincidence, while the crystallographic indices $(1 1 1)$ specify the boundary plane orientation. This relationship describes a symmetric tilt grain boundary in a cubic crystal system, where the boundary plane is parallel to the close-packed $\{111\}$ family of planes. The formulation captures the geometric condition for atomic registry at the interface, which influences the boundary's energy and defect structure.

The notation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ denotes a specific type of structural unit (SU) in a grain boundary. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the interface, a common parameter for classifying grain boundary geometry. The value $\Sigma = 3$ indicates a coincidence site lattice (CSL) with a periodicity where one in three lattice sites is coincident between the two adjoining crystals. The Miller indices $(1 1 1)$ specify the crystallographic plane in which the boundary predominantly lies. Therefore, this equation formally defines a symmetric tilt grain boundary characterized by a $\Sigma 3$ CSL relationship on a $\{111\}$ plane, which is a prevalent and low-energy interface in face-centered cubic materials, often associated with a coherent twin boundary.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ describes a specific type of grain boundary in a crystalline material. Here, the symbol $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites (the sigma value) at the interface between two misoriented crystal grains. The notation $1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ specifies the crystallographic parameters of this boundary: the sigma value is 11, indicating a 1/11 fraction of lattice sites are coincident between the two grains, and the boundary plane is the $(1 1 3)$ crystallographic plane. This equation thus defines a coincidence site lattice (CSL) grain boundary characterized by a $\Sigma 11$ misorientation relationship with a $(113)$ boundary plane.

The notation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 2 )$ denotes a specific type of crystallographic grain boundary, characterized by its coincident site lattice (CSL) index. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites at the interface, which in this case has a value of 3. The Miller indices $(1\,1\,2)$ following the slash specify the crystallographic plane in which the grain boundary lies. This equation thus defines a symmetric tilt grain boundary with a $\Sigma 3$ misorientation relationship about the $\langle 1\,1\,2 \rangle$ axis, where the boundary plane is normal to the $(1\,1\,2)$ plane. Such a description is standard in materials science for classifying the geometry and energy of interfaces in polycrystalline materials.

The equation $\Sigma = 17/(223)$ defines the reciprocal density, $\Sigma$, for a specific grain boundary. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal coincidence site lattice (CSL) density, which characterizes the geometric periodicity at the interface between two misoriented crystals. The value $17/(223)$ represents the ratio of the volume (or area) of the coincidence lattice unit cell to that of the crystal lattice unit cell, quantifying the degree of atomic matching across the boundary. This particular $\Sigma$ value corresponds to a grain boundary with a misorientation angle of approximately $86.63^\circ$, as indicated in the context, and is associated with a specific three-dimensional CSL configuration.

The equation $\Sigma = 11/(113)$ describes a geometric parameter in the crystallographic characterization of a grain boundary (GB). Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites, a key metric in the coincidence site lattice (CSL) model used to classify the misorientation and boundary plane between two adjoining crystals. The specific value of $\Sigma = 11$ indicates that one in eleven lattice sites from the two crystals are coincident, while the Miller indices $(113)$ specify the crystallographic plane on which the grain boundary lies. This relationship defines a particular symmetric tilt boundary configuration, where the boundary plane and misorientation are linked through the CSL formalism. The provided context further elaborates that this $\Sigma 11/(113)$ structural unit (SU) interacts with other SUs, such as $\Sigma 3/(111)$ units, within the complex atomic structure of the grain boundary.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes a grain boundary (GB) structural unit (SU) characterized by its coincident site lattice (CSL) notation. In this notation, the symbol $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, which in this case has a value of 3. The term $(1 1 1)$ represents the Miller indices of the boundary plane, indicating that the GB plane is oriented along the $\{111\}$ family of crystallographic planes. This equation thus specifies a low-angle or special GB configuration where the misorientation between the two grains results in a coincidence site lattice with a periodicity of 3, and the interface lies on a close-packed $\{111\}$ plane. The context indicates that such $\Sigma = 3/(111)$ structural units are integral components of more complex GBs and are associated with the formation of stacking faults and lattice plane bending in the interboundary region.

The equation $\Sigma = 11/(113)$ defines a coincident site lattice (CSL) grain boundary descriptor. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, a dimensionless integer parameter characterizing the geometric periodicity of the bicrystal interface. The Miller indices $(113)$ specify the crystallographic plane that forms the boundary plane. The relationship $\Sigma = 11$ indicates that the supercell of the coincident lattice contains 11 atomic sites from one crystal for every site that is coincident with the second crystal, thereby quantifying the degree of lattice matching and the structural unit period at the interface.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes a coincident site lattice (CSL) notation for a grain boundary in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, which characterizes the geometric periodicity of the boundary. The specific value $\Sigma = 3$ indicates that one in every three lattice sites is coincident between the two adjoining crystals. The denominator $(1 1 1)$ represents the Miller indices of the crystallographic plane in which the boundary predominantly lies. Thus, this equation defines a symmetric tilt grain boundary on the {111} plane with a periodicity corresponding to $\Sigma 3$, a configuration commonly associated with low energy and specific defect structures such as stacking faults.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ defines a geometric parameter for a grain boundary in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites, a key metric in the coincident site lattice (CSL) model used to characterize the misorientation between two adjacent crystal grains. The notation $1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ specifies the specific CSL configuration, where the numerical values correspond to crystallographic indices. In this context, the equation describes a particular symmetric tilt grain boundary, where the boundary plane is oriented along the $(1 1 3)$ crystallographic plane and the misorientation between the grains results in a $\Sigma 11$ CSL relationship. This parameter is central to classifying the atomic structure and energy of the interface.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ denotes the sigma (Σ) value for a specific grain boundary in a crystalline material. Here, Σ represents the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the boundary, a parameter used to classify the geometric periodicity of grain boundary structures. The notation $3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ specifies that this is a Σ3 boundary with a {111} crystallographic plane as the boundary plane. This equation characterizes a symmetric tilt grain boundary where the misorientation between the two grains corresponds to a Σ3 coincidence site lattice (CSL) relationship, and the boundary itself lies along the {111} plane. In the provided context, this specific Σ3/(111) boundary is associated with a downward bending of the {111} lattice planes in the adjacent crystal regions.

The equation $\Sigma = 11/(113)$ describes a crystallographic notation used to characterize a grain boundary. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, a parameter that quantifies the geometric misfit between two adjacent crystalline grains. The notation $11/(113)$ specifies the Miller indices of the boundary plane, where $(113)$ represents the crystallographic plane on which the boundary lies. This relationship indicates that the boundary is a coincident site lattice (CSL) boundary with a $\Sigma 11$ misorientation, and its interface is oriented along the $\{113\}$ family of planes. Such notation is commonly employed in materials science to classify the structure and energy of grain boundaries based on their geometric configuration.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ denotes a specific singular grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, a parameter used to characterize the geometric misfit at a grain boundary. The value $\Sigma = 3$ indicates a coincidence site lattice (CSL) with a periodicity where one in three lattice sites is coincident between the two adjoining crystals. The notation "$/ ( 1 1 1 )$" specifies the boundary plane orientation, which in this case is the crystallographic {111} plane. This equation thus fully defines a symmetric tilt boundary with a specific misorientation angle, where the boundary plane is parallel to a close-packed {111} plane, a configuration known for its low energy and singular behavior.

The equation $\Sigma = 11/(113)$ describes the crystallographic designation of a grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites (the Sigma value) at the boundary, which is a metric for the degree of geometric coincidence between the two adjoining crystal lattices. The notation $11/(113)$ specifies the specific GB plane; the number 11 refers to the Sigma value, while the Miller indices $(113)$ define the crystallographic orientation of the boundary plane relative to the crystal lattice. This designation indicates a singular, low-energy GB configuration. The relationship captured by the equation is that the boundary is characterized by a coincidence site lattice (CSL) with a Sigma value of 11 and lies on a plane of the {113} family, which together define its specific crystallographic character and energy state.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material using the coincidence site lattice (CSL) model. Here, the symbol $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincidence sites, which in this case has a value of 3. The notation $/( 1 1 1 )$ specifies the crystallographic plane of the boundary, indicating that the GB plane is parallel to the $(111)$ family of lattice planes. This equation thus defines a singular, low-energy $\Sigma 3$ boundary with a $(111)$ orientation, which serves as a reference structure. As indicated in the context, boundaries that are vicinal (slightly misoriented) to this singular GB can be described by introducing steps while largely preserving the low-energy core structure of this $\Sigma 3/(111)$ configuration.

The equation $\Sigma = 33$ defines the reciprocal density of coincident sites, or Sigma value, for a specific grain boundary. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites at the interface, a key parameter in the coincident site lattice (CSL) model used to describe the crystallographic geometry of grain boundaries. In this context, the equation specifies that the boundary under consideration is a $\Sigma 33$ grain boundary, which has a misorientation of approximately $8.51^\circ$ from the $\Sigma 11/(113)$ misorientation, as indicated by the associated Miller index notation /~225!. This CSL designation characterizes the periodicity and potential energy of the interface.

Equation (1) expresses the magnitude of the Burgers vector, \(\left| \mathbf{b}_{\text{SGBD}} \right|\), associated with a set of secondary grain boundary dislocations (SGBDs) required to accommodate a deviation from a reference coincidence site lattice misorientation. In this relation, \(D\) denotes the spacing between adjacent SGBDs in the boundary, and \(\Delta \theta\) represents the angular deviation from the exact coincidence misorientation. The equation is derived from a modified Read-Shockley-Frank formulation for low-angle boundaries, where the sine of half the misorientation angle, scaled by twice the dislocation spacing, gives the magnitude of the Burgers vector content per unit length of boundary. This geometrically relates the density and strength of the dislocations to the local crystallographic misfit they accommodate.

The equation $\mathbf{b}_{\mathrm{SGBD}} = \frac{2}{11}[113]$ defines the total Burgers vector of the secondary grain boundary dislocations (SGBDs) required to accommodate a specific deviation from a reference grain boundary. Here, $\mathbf{b}_{\mathrm{SGBD}}$ denotes the Burgers vector, which is expressed in crystallographic terms. The vector $[113]$ represents the crystallographic direction of the Burgers vector in the crystal lattice, while the prefactor $\frac{2}{11}$ scales its magnitude. This specific form results from applying a modified Read-Shockley-Frank formula to a boundary that deviates by a small angle $\Delta \theta$ from a $\Sigma = 11/(113)$ coincidence site lattice misorientation. The equation thus quantifies the net dislocation content needed to describe the observed boundary structure.

The notation $\Sigma = 33/(225)$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites (the Sigma value) for a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites at the interface, which characterizes the geometric periodicity and symmetry of the boundary. The integers 33 and (225) specify the crystallographic parameters defining the boundary plane and misorientation; the denominator (225) indicates the Miller indices of the boundary plane, while the numerator 33 is related to the misorientation angle and axis. This equation thus defines a particular high-angle symmetric tilt grain boundary, where the low $\Sigma$ value signifies a relatively high density of coincident lattice sites and suggests a boundary with reduced energy and specific structural units.

The equation $\Sigma = 11/(113)$ defines the reciprocal density of coincidence sites, denoted by $\Sigma$, for a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the inverse fraction of lattice sites that are coincident between two misoriented crystals, a key parameter in the coincidence site lattice (CSL) model of grain boundaries. The notation $(113)$ indicates the Miller indices of the boundary plane that is perpendicular to the tilt axis. This particular $\Sigma=11$ boundary with a $(113)$ plane is referenced as a fundamental structural unit, where its periodic displacement vector serves as a building block for describing more complex boundary geometries, such as the decomposed $\Sigma=33/(225)$ boundary discussed in the context.

The equation expresses a decomposition of a periodic vector associated with a grain boundary (GB) into components representing structural units. Here, the term \( \frac{1}{2}[554] \) denotes the periodic vector in the boundary plane for the \(\Sigma = 33/(225)\) GB. This vector is decomposed as the sum of \( \frac{3}{4}[332] \), which corresponds to one and a half periods of the \(\Sigma = 11/(113)\) GB periodic vector, and \( \frac{1}{4}[112] \), representing a step that is half a period of the \(\Sigma = 3/(111)\) GB. The relationship illustrates how the structure of the higher-\(\Sigma\) boundary can be constructed from units of a lower-\(\Sigma\) boundary and a step containing a secondary grain boundary dislocation (SGBD).

The equation $\Sigma = 33/(225)$ defines the reciprocal density of coincidence sites, denoted by $\Sigma$, for a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal of the density of lattice sites that are coincident between two misoriented crystals, a key parameter in the structural unit model of grain boundaries. The notation $(225)$ refers to the Miller indices of the boundary plane, which is perpendicular to the tilt axis. This particular $\Sigma$ value characterizes a symmetric tilt grain boundary whose periodic structure can be decomposed into structural units from related boundaries, as described by the decomposition of its periodic displacement vector.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ defines a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material, where $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the interface. In this notation, the integer 11 represents the sigma value, which quantifies the degree of geometric coincidence between the two adjoining crystal lattices. The crystallographic indices (113) specify the boundary plane common to both grains. This equation therefore characterizes a symmetric tilt grain boundary with a specific misorientation and interface orientation, serving as a structural unit in the analysis of more complex boundary geometries, as referenced in the decomposition of the $\Sigma = 33/(225)$ GB.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ defines a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material, where the sigma ($\Sigma$) value denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the interface. In this notation, $\Sigma = 3$ indicates a coincidence site lattice (CSL) with a periodicity three times that of the bulk crystal lattice. The Miller indices $(1 1 1)$ specify the crystallographic plane in which the boundary lies, which in this case is the close-packed {111} plane. This GB is characterized as a low-angle or special high-angle boundary, and its structural period serves as a fundamental unit in describing more complex boundary configurations, such as those containing steps or secondary grain boundary dislocations.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ describes a specific grain boundary geometry in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites, a parameter used to characterize the periodicity and energy of a grain boundary. The notation $1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ specifies the crystallographic indices, where the Miller indices $(1 1 3)$ define the boundary plane orientation relative to the crystal lattices of the adjoining grains. This particular $\Sigma$ value indicates a high degree of atomic coincidence across the interface, corresponding to a geometrically ideal, low-energy configuration. As noted in the context, boundaries with this $\Sigma$ value can form structurally ordered units (SU's) that approximate random-close-packed arrangements, making them sensitive to elastic distortions, such as those induced by localized dislocation cores.

The equation $\Sigma { = } 1 1 / ( 1 1 3 )$ defines a specific grain boundary (GB) in a crystalline material, characterized by its coincident site lattice (CSL) designation. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, a key parameter in classifying symmetric tilt grain boundaries. The notation $(1 1 3)$ specifies the Miller indices of the boundary plane. This particular $\Sigma 11$ boundary with a $(113)$ plane orientation represents a structural unit (SU) within a complex grain boundary structure. The equation thus formally identifies a specific, high-angle symmetric tilt grain boundary configuration, which is used in the analysis of interfacial energy and atomic structure at crystalline interfaces.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ describes a specific type of structural unit (SU) in a grain boundary. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the interface, a parameter characterizing the geometric periodicity of the bicrystal. The expression $3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ specifies that this $\Sigma 3$ boundary has a {111} crystallographic plane as its boundary plane. This configuration corresponds to a coherent twin boundary, where the atomic arrangement at the interface is nearly perfect and exhibits a low energy. The context indicates that this SU possesses an ideal misorientation angle of 39°, which serves as a reference state for analyzing the local bending of atomic planes near grain boundary defects.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ denotes a specific grain boundary (GB) structural unit (SU) in a crystalline material. Here, $\Sigma$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the boundary, a parameter characterizing the geometric periodicity of the interface. The notation $3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ indicates that this SU corresponds to a $\Sigma 3$ misorientation relationship about the $\langle 1 1 1 \rangle$ crystallographic axis. This structural unit is associated with an ideal misorientation angle of $39^\circ$ and serves as a fundamental building block in the vicinal description of asymmetric tilt grain boundaries, where its local arrangement influences the boundary's energy and structure.

The expression $\Sigma { = } 1 7 / ( 2 2 3 )$ defines a specific grain boundary (GB) character, where the sigma ($\Sigma$) notation denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the interface. In this notation, $\Sigma$ represents the inverse fraction of lattice sites that are coincident between the two adjoining crystals, and the Miller indices $(2 2 3)$ specify the crystallographic plane of the boundary. This particular $\Sigma 17 / (2 2 3)$ GB is a high-angle boundary characterized by a specific geometric misorientation and plane, which can be analyzed within the framework of the coincident site lattice (CSL) model. The context indicates that, despite its formal CSL description, the local atomic structure of this boundary may be more intuitively understood as being vicinal to a lower-$\Sigma$ reference boundary, such as $\Sigma 3 / (1 1 1)$.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 1 )$ defines a specific grain boundary (GB) crystallographic character. Here, $\Sigma$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the boundary, a key parameter in the coincident site lattice (CSL) model for describing interfacial structure. The notation indicates a $\Sigma 3$ boundary with a crystallographic plane normal of $(111)$. This describes a symmetric tilt or twist boundary where the misorientation between the two adjoining crystals results in one out of every three lattice sites being coincident, and the boundary plane is oriented along the close-packed $\{111\}$ family. In the provided context, such boundaries are discussed as a reference structure for analyzing relaxation mechanisms in materials with low stacking fault energy.

The equation $\sum = 3 / ( 1 1 2 )$ describes the crystallographic orientation relationship for a grain boundary. Here, $\sum$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites, a parameter used to characterize the geometric periodicity at the interface between two misoriented crystals. The notation $3 / ( 1 1 2 )$ specifies that this is a $\sum 3$ boundary with a $( 1 1 2 )$ boundary plane. This particular configuration corresponds to an incoherent twin boundary in face-centered cubic materials such as aluminum, where the two adjoining grains share a specific misorientation but the boundary plane is not the typical coherent twin plane.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 2 )$ denotes the crystallographic designation of a grain boundary (GB) within a crystalline material. Here, $\sum$ represents the reciprocal density of coincident lattice sites, a parameter used to classify symmetric tilt boundaries. The value $3$ specifies the sigma number, indicating the periodicity of the coincidence site lattice. The notation $(1 1 2)$ refers to the Miller indices of the crystallographic plane that constitutes the boundary plane. This equation thus defines a specific high-angle symmetric tilt grain boundary, known as an incoherent twin boundary, where the interface is oriented along the $\{112\}$ family of planes with a coincidence site lattice of order three.

The equation $\scriptstyle \sum = 3 / ( 1 1 2 )$ describes a specific grain boundary in a crystalline material. Here, $\sum$ denotes the reciprocal density of coincident sites at the boundary, a parameter used to classify the geometric periodicity of the interface. The value 3 is the sigma number, which indicates that one in every three lattice sites is coincident between the two adjoining crystals. The term $(1 1 2)$ refers to the Miller indices of the boundary plane, which in this case is the {112} crystallographic plane. This combination defines an incoherent twin boundary with a specific misorientation and interface orientation.
